s.def: Japan (a country and archipelago of East Asia)

japan.e: From Malay Jepang, from Hokkien 日本 (Ji̍t-pún), from Middle Chinese 日本 (nyit pwon^X, literally “sun origin”).

s.def: Jamaica (an island and country in the Caribbean)

jupiter.e: From Middle Dutch jupiter, from Latin Iūpiter.

s.def: yacht (fast, light and now often luxurious (sailing) boat)
examples: Hij kocht een nieuw jacht voor zijn zomervakantie.
Ze vaarden op hun jacht naar het Caribisch gebied.

jacht.e: From Middle Dutch jacht, jaecht, jaget, from Old Dutch *jagot, from Proto-West Germanic *jagōþu (“hunt, hunting”). Equivalent to jagen (“to hunt”) + -t (“verbal noun”). Cognate with German Jagd. The form jaecht is due to contraction and jacht to subsequent pre-cluster shortening. (Compare maagd, ...

s.def: young people who are considered unsophisticated

jargon.e: From Old French jargon (“chatter, talk, language”).

s.def: the tenth letter of the Dutch alphabet
the tenth letter of the Dutch alphabet

examples: As for the puma and the jaguar, it clearly appears from the descriptions by them, who have seen these animals, that the cougar is not a lion, and [that] the jaguar is not a tiger; […]

jaguar.e: Apparently borrowed from French jaguar, from Portuguese jaguar, from Old Tupi îagûara. The contemporary pronunciation derives from English jaguar.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almelo, Overijssel, Netherlands

java.e: The neighbourhood is named after the Indonesian island.

s.def: Johannesburg (a city, the provincial capital of Gauteng, South Africa)

johannesburg.e: From Johannes + -burg. As precise records for the choice of name were lost and there were quite a number of people with the name Johannes who were involved in the early history of the city, it is not known which person the city was named after. See Johannesburg § Etymology on Wikipedia.

s.def: a male given name, equivalent to English John

jan.e: From Middle Dutch jan, from Latin Iōhannēs, from Ancient Greek Ἰωάννης (Iōánnēs), from Hebrew יוֹחָנָן. Shortening of Johannes.

judo.e: Borrowed from Japanese 柔(じゅう)道(どう) (jūdō).

examples: Wat doe je daar?
Ik doe dit wel voor je.
Hoe gaat het met je? — Goed. En met jou?

s.def: subjective unstressed form of jullie (“you (plural), y'all”)
examples: Wat doe je daar?

s.def: objective unstressed form of jullie (“you (plural), y'all”)
examples: Ik doe dit wel voor je.

s.def: one, people, you, someone, anyone; an unspecified individual or group of individuals (as subject or object)
examples: Je mag hier niet zwemmen.
In dat restaurant kun je heerlijk eten.

s.def: I, one; used to talk about oneself indirectly, especially about feelings or personal experiences
examples: Je gaat wel even een moeilijk periode door, maar je zoekt toch naar een oplossing.

Het echtpaar Duijm uit Spijkenisse toog zo'n vier maanden geleden naar de Kamer van Koophandel omdat meneer en mevrouw wel wat zagen in een strijkservice. (...) „Voor ƒ 58 inschrijfgeld waren we plotseling directeur en directrice. Na afloop voel je weer de frisse lucht buiten en dan denk je wel even: waar zijn we aan begonnen?"

s.def: possessive unstressed form of jij (“you (singular)”): your (singular)
examples: Neem je boek en maak die oefening.

s.def: possessive unstressed form of jullie (“you (plural), y'all”): your (plural), y'all's
examples: Neem je boeken en maak die oefening.

juni.e: From Latin Iūniī, genitive of Iūnius.

s.def: Job (the eighteenth book of the Old Testament)

examples: Wil je met ons meegaan? — Ja, graag!
Nee heb je al, ja kan je krijgen.
Het begon met een ja...
"Ja!" riep hij luid toen er een doelpunt viel.

s.def: A casual greeting acknowledging the presence of a person; hey, hi, what's up
Used as a request for someone to repeat something not heard or understood clearly; say again, what's that

ja.e: From Middle Dutch ja, from Old Dutch *jā, from Proto-Germanic *ja.

s.def: you; second-person singular subjective personal pronoun
examples: Jij was er niet.

jij.e:
From Middle Dutch ji, northern form of gi, from Old Dutch gī, from Proto-Germanic *jīz, a northwest Germanic variant of *jūz, from Proto-Indo-European *yúHs. Cognate with Low German ji, jie, English ye, West Frisian jimme, German ihr.
Until the earlier 20th century, jij was considered exclusively...

s.def: jam (congealed sweet mixture of conserved fruits)
someone who hasn't been born and raised in Volendam
a jack or knave, especially as a trump card
alternative letter-case form of jas (“jack, knave”)

jas.e: Origin unknown. Compare West Frisian jas (“coat, jacket”), Saterland Frisian Jasse (“jacket”), Middle Low German jesse, gesse (“a man's garment, doublet, jacket”).

s.def: any wild card or similar, even in non-card games
junior (younger or lower-ranked person, for example in job titles)
a male given name from Hebrew, equivalent to English Jacob or James
yak, the ox-like Himalayan bovine, Bos grunniens

jak.e: From Tibetan གཡག (g.yag), from Proto-Sino-Tibetan *g-jak ~ g-jaŋ.

januari.e: From Latin Iānuāriī, genitive of Iānuārius.

s.def: joint, marijuana cigarette (generally larger than a stickie)
Used to address someone, somewhat like dude or mate.

examples: Ey! - Jo! - Hey! - Hi!
Later! - Jo! - Later! - Bye!
Fijn weekend! - Jo! - Have a nice weekend! - You too!

s.def: a diminutive of the female given names Johanna or Josefien

jute.e: Borrowed from English jute, from Bengali ঝুট (jhuṭ). The Dutch pronunciation is a spelling pronunciation.

s.def: a male given name, equivalent to English John

jury.e: Borrowed from English jury or French jury (itself from English), from Middle English jure, from Anglo-Norman juree, from Medieval Latin iūrāta. If the Dutch term was not directly borrowed from French, the pronunciation has been secondarily influenced by the French pronunciation.

examples: Het eerste gevecht was een aanval op een detachement door vele duizenden der Burmesen, in den mond van een jungle, waerdoor zy gedekt waren.

jungle.e: Borrowed from English jungle, from Hindi जंगल (jaṅgal) and Urdu جنگل (jangal), from Sanskrit जङ्गल (jaṅgala, “arid, sterile, desert”).

examples: Ik snap er geen jota van.

jota.e: From Ancient Greek ἰῶτα (iôta). Doublet of jod. The sense "small amount" developed in reference to a phrase in the New Testament: Eer de hemel en aarde vergaat, zal er niet één jota of één tittel vergaan van de wet.— until heaven and earth pass away, not an iota, not a dot, will pass from the Law...

s.def: a diminutive of the female given name Jo
examples: (extract uit De Leidse sleutelgaten's song "Joke stop toch met koken"): Joke, stop toch met koken. Kom uit de keuken, mijn lieve Joke. Stop toch met koken, kom uit de keuken. Want ik wil gezellig samen met je neu…tronenbommenstickers op m’n nieuwe tas gaan plakken.
Ik bel je later. -Joe.
Mag ik je pen lenen? -Alsjeblieft. -Joe!
Onze tegenstanders zijn een stel janetten.

janet.e: Borrowed from French janet, from Jeannette, from Jeanne d'Arc. Named after the members of a Roman Catholic girlscouts' organisation.

s.def: Yugoslavia (a former country in Southeast Europe in the Balkans, now split into 6 countries: Bosnia and Herzegovina, Croatia, North Macedonia, Montenegro, Serbia, and Slovenia)
an expression of surprise: gosh, golly, gee

jee.e: Contracted form of Jezus, used as a minced oath.

s.def: jest; frivolous, unserious intent or mood
Yemen (a country in West Asia in the Middle East)
a jingle (song segment used in a commercial or radio program; also used for certain other sound samples used by radio DJs)

jogger.e: Borrowed from English jogger, equivalent to joggen + -er.

jesse.e: From Ancient Greek Ἰεσσαί (Iessaí), from Hebrew יִשַׁי (Yishai).

jammer.e: From Middle Dutch jammer, from Old Dutch iāmer, from Proto-Germanic *jēmaraz (“miserable, sorrowful”).

s.def: year (the time it takes the Earth to circle the Sun; its equivalent on other planets)
examples: Een jaar op Mars duurt ongeveer 687 aardse dagen.
Het is bijna een jaar geleden dat we elkaar zagen.

s.def: year (the individuals associated with a year, notably the students in or graduates from an annual class at school)
examples: Het jaar 2023 zal hun afstudeerjaar zijn.
Alle studenten van het jaar 2020 zijn uitgenodigd voor de reünie.

jaar.e: From Middle Dutch jâer, from Old Dutch jār, from Proto-Germanic *jērą, from Proto-Indo-European *yeh₁-.

s.def: a young: a young being, especially an immature animal

jong.e: From Middle Dutch jonc, from Old Dutch junc, from Proto-West Germanic *jung, from Proto-Germanic *jungaz, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂yuh₁n̥ḱós. Compare German jung, English young, Danish ung, Icelandic ungur.

s.def: to tug (a boat) (not by other vessels)

jagen.e: From Middle Dutch jāgen, from Old Dutch *jagon, from Proto-West Germanic *jagōn.

examples: De jongen speelt in de tuin met zijn bal.

s.def: members of a male 'peers' group, e.g. a military unit
Informal term used to address multiple children of masculine or mixed gender; kids, guys (may be considered insufficiently gender-inclusive)
examples: Kom op, jongens, laten we naar buiten gaan en spelen!

s.def: Colloquial term used by men to address other familiar men; mate, dude, bro
examples: Hé, jongens, hoe gaat het vandaag?

s.def: a male servant, especially but not exclusively a younger one (using it for older men may be dated and offensive)
examples: De rijke koopman had een jongen die hem hielp bij zijn dagelijkse taken.

s.def: a male trainee or newcomer in a job
examples: De nieuwe medewerker is nog maar een jongen in het vak, dus we moeten hem nog veel leren.

s.def: to give birth, to have offspring, i.e. to deliver one or more young

jongen.e: From Middle Dutch jongen, dative/accusative form of jonge, a nominal use of jong (“young”). Cognate with German Junge (“boy”).

s.def: a male given name from Late Latin Iōhannēs [in turn from Koine Greek Ἰωάννης (Iōánnēs), in turn from Biblical Hebrew יוֹחָנָן (Yôḥānān, “God is gracious”)], equivalent to English John

johannes.e: From Late Latin Iōhannēs, variant of Latin Iōannēs, from Koine Greek Ἰωάννης (Iōánnēs), from Biblical Hebrew יוֹחָנָן (Yôḥānān). Cognate with English John.

s.def: cup (like a cup of coffee or tea)

jat.e: Borrowed from Yiddish יד (yad, “hand”) from Hebrew יָד (yāḏ, “hand”).

jurk.e:
Uncertain. Possibly shortened from earlier *jurreken (compare English jerkin), assimilated from *jurneken, from Middle Dutch jo(u)rney, journede (“surcoat”), from Old French journée, formed from jor (“day (of the tournament)”), with the suffix -ade (“at, from or before”).
In the sense 'clothing' ...

s.def: a surname originating as a patronymic
a male given name, the Latinized form of Jacob
James (regnal name of Scottish, English and Spanish monarchs)

examples: Iö den dappren wever! / De vederbos knikt op zijn hoed; / Hij ademt wraak, heeft dorst naar bloed, / En lescht dien met jenever.

jenever.e: From Middle Dutch jenever, genever, geniver, from Old French genevre, from Vulgar Latin ziniperus, from Latin iūniperus (“juniper”).

s.def: Jordan (a country in West Asia in the Middle East)
you; second-person plural subjective personal pronoun
examples: Zijn jullie klaar?

s.def: you; second-person plural objective personal pronoun
examples: Ik zal het aan jullie geven
Dit zal niet werken voor jullie
Jullie leraar is ziek.

jullie.e: From earlier jelui, from jij (“you”) and lui (“people”).

s.def: you; second-person singular objective personal pronoun
examples: Ik zal dit wel even doen voor jou.
Kan ik jou iets vragen?

jou.e: From Middle Dutch jou, from Old Dutch *jū, a northern (Frisian?) variant of *iu, from Proto-Germanic *iwwiz, a West Germanic variant of *izwiz. Doublet of u.

s.def: a male given name, equivalent to English Jerome or Jeremy

jeroen.e: Ultimately from Latin Hieronymus, from Ancient Greek Ῑ̔ερώνῠμος (Hīerṓnŭmos).

s.def: a burden; something which represses or restrains a person

juk.e: From Middle Dutch joc, juc, from Old Dutch *juk, from Proto-Germanic *juką, from Proto-Indo-European *yugóm. Compare German Joch, West Frisian jok, English yoke, Danish åg, Swedish ok.

s.def: in thin strips, in the manner of julienne
the cutting technique of cutting food into thin strips
julienne, French cut (food that has been cut in julienne)

jaloezie.e: From Middle Dutch jalosie, from Old French jalosie, from jalous.

s.def: a female given name from Latin, masculine equivalent Johannes, equivalent to English Jane

johanna.e: From Middle Dutch Johanna, from Latin Iōhannā, from Ancient Greek Ἰωάννᾱ (Iōánnā), from Hebrew יוֹחָנָה.

s.def: a jerrycan or similar container, used for fuel or other liquids (especially drinking water) and made of plastic or metal

jouw.e: From Middle Dutch jouwe, from Old Dutch *jūwa, a northern (Frisian?) variant of *iuwa, from Proto-West Germanic *iuwar, from Proto-Germanic *izweraz. Doublet of uw.

judea.e: From Middle Dutch judea, from Latin Iūdaea, from Ancient Greek Ἰουδαία (Ioudaía), from Biblical Hebrew יְהוּדָה (yehudá).

s.def: Jericho (a city in the West Bank, Palestine)

Judah, Judea (a traditional region and ancient kingdom (c. 930 — 586 BCE) in the Land of Israel in modern Israel and Palestine; the southern Israelite kingdom which continued to be ruled by the Davidic dynasty after Solomon's death and the breakup of the united monarchy, with the northern portion keeping the name Israel)

juda.e: Ultimately from Ancient Greek Ἰουδά (Ioudá), from Biblical Hebrew יְהוּדָה.

s.def: a male given name, equivalent to English Caspar

jasper.e: From Spanish Gaspar, from Biblical Hebrew גִּזְבָּר (gizbar), ultimately from Old Median *ganǰabarah. First recorded in 1627, with other spellings Gaspar (1626), Jespaer and Jesper (1628).

examples: Ik kwam juist terug van mijn werk, toen ik een ongeluk zag gebeuren.
Zo’n laffe uitvlucht van jou is juist wat ik niet wilde horen.

s.def: conversely, on the other hand; actually; indeed; even (emphasizing a contrast)
examples: Katten houden niet van water, terwijl honden er juist gek op zijn.
Ze zei dat ik mijn schuchterheid nog een stukje kwijt moest raken, maar ik had juist alles gedaan om kalm en professioneel te lijken.

juist.e: From Middle Dutch juust, from Old French juste, from Latin iūstus, from Proto-Italic *jowestos, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₂yew-. More at just.

jongeling.e: Diachronically from Middle Dutch jongelinc, from Old Dutch iungelig, from Proto-Germanic *jungilingaz. By surface analysis, jong + -ling.

jonas.e: From Middle Dutch Jonas, from Latin Iōnās, from Ancient Greek Ἰωνᾶς (Iōnâs), from Biblical Hebrew יוֹנָה.

s.def: a male given name, variant of Jacob

examples: Het bedrijf heeft zojuist haar jaarverslag gepubliceerd, waarin de financiële resultaten van het afgelopen jaar worden gepresenteerd.
Het jaarverslag van de non-profitorganisatie toont de impact van hun werk op de gemeenschap.

s.def: a male given name, equivalent to English Joachim

jeuken.e:
From Middle Dutch joken, juecken, long-vowel variants of jocken, jucken, from Old Dutch *jukken, from Proto-West Germanic *jukkjan. The long vowels developed before the single consonants in the 2nd and 3rd person singular forms, e.g. Old Dutch 3rd person singular *jukit.
Cognate with German jucke...

examples: Kunt u iets aan de jeuk doen?

jeugd.e: From Middle Dutch joghet, juegt, from Old Dutch jugint, jugethe, from Proto-Germanic *jugunþiz, from earlier *juwunþi.

s.def: Jutland (a cultural region and peninsula in northwestern Europe, consisting of the mainland part of Denmark and Schleswig-Holstein, which is part of Germany)
person whose birthday it is; birthday girl, birthday boy; birthday kid
a male given name from Hebrew of Hebrew origin.

examples: Die jood heeft me genaaid, 200 euro naar de klote, godverkanker.

s.def: a Jew, a member of the Jewish people
superseded spelling of jood (“adherent of Judaism”)

jood.e:
From Middle Dutch jōde, from Old Dutch judo, from Latin iūdaeus, from Ancient Greek Ἰουδαῖος (Ioudaîos).
The soccer sense ("supporter of AFC Ajax") refers to the fact that the club Ajax hails from Amsterdam, a city with a traditionally large Jewish community. While supporters from rivaling clubs ...

juut.e: From jood, with the connotation to attempt to gain an unfair price in a business deal. It is used because the police fine people as a punishment.

s.def: jackal, one of certain canids of the genus Canis

jakhals.e: Borrowed from another European language, ultimately from Turkish çakal, from Persian شغال (šaġâl), from Sanskrit सृगाल (sṛgālá, “jackal”). Modified into the current form by folk etymology.

s.def: said to a horse to make it start moving
Jehovah (name for God derived from vocalisation of the Tetragrammaton)
jackfruit (a tree, Artocarpus heterophyllus, of the Moraceae family, which produces edible fruit)
jackfruit (the large fruit from this tree)

juliana.e: Borrowed from Latin Iūliāna, feminine form of Iūliānus.

s.def: a female given name from French
tenner, banknote of ten monetary units (usually guilders or euros)

joet.e: Borrowed from Yiddish י (yud, “yod (tenth letter of the alphabet)”), from Hebrew י.

s.def: skua, any bird of the genus Stercorarius

jager.e: From Middle Dutch jager, from Old Dutch jageri. Equivalent to jagen + -er.

juichen.e: Cognate with German jauchzen. The further etymology is not entirely clear. If the Dutch and German words are inherited from a common source, the word would be of Proto-West Germanic origin at the very latest, although it has been suggested that the Dutch word could represent a borrowing from Germ...

s.def: a diminutive of the female given name Johanna

examples: De vereniging organiseert een jaarlijkse bijeenkomst voor haar leden.
Het jaarlijkse festival trekt elk jaar duizenden bezoekers.
Het festival vindt jaarlijks plaats in de zomer.
Deze conferentie wordt jaarlijks georganiseerd door de branchevereniging.

jaarlijks.e: From Middle Dutch jaerlijcs, from earlier jaerlijc, which is cognate with English yearly. Equivalent to jaar + -lijks.

s.def: a Belgian municipality, at the north side of Brussels
an emphatic yes, to indicate strong agreement, or dissent in reply to a negative statement or question
examples: "Jawel", zei ze, "ik denk wel dat dat juist is."
Je hebt je huiswerk niet gemaakt. — Jawel, ik heb mijn huiswerk wel gemaakt!

jawel.e: Univerbation of ja (“yes”) + wel (“well, indeed”). Compare German jawohl.

juwelier.e: From Middle French joaillier. Equivalent to juweel + -ier.

s.def: a personal attack, especially a tu quoque
a male given name, shortened form of Joachim
a diminutive of the male given name Jacob

jaap.e: Probably related to japen (“to cut deeply”), which could be a dialectal variant of gapen (“to yawn, open up”).

examples: De jongens spelen voetbal in het park.

s.def: guys, a form of address for a group of male persons or a group of mixed male and female persons
examples: Kom op, jongens, we moeten aan het werk.
Jongens, wat een grote vis!

jesaja.e: From older Esaias, from Latin Ēsāiās or Ancient Greek Ἠσαΐας (Ēsaḯas), from Biblical Hebrew יְשַׁעְיָהוּ. The later addition of the initial J was influenced by the original Hebrew name.

jeremia.e: From Middle Dutch jeremias, from Latin Jeremias, from Ancient Greek Ἰερεμίας (Ieremías), from Biblical Hebrew יִרְמְיָה.

s.def: Japanese (inhabitant of Japan; person of Japanese descent)
Joel, the Biblical prophet (and eponymous Bible book)
a male given name, equivalent to English Joel
gout, a metabolic disease which painfully inflames the joints

jicht.e: From Middle Dutch jicht, from Old Dutch *jiht, *giht, from Proto-Germanic *jihtiz.

examples: De Gregoriaanse jaartelling wordt wereldwijd gebruikt.
Verschillende culturen hebben verschillende jaartellingen.

s.def: hunting dog, hound (dog used for hunting)
you know (could be seen as the Dutch equivalent of "you know what I'm sayin'")

examples:
Normally used in conjunction with the definite article de or het depending on the gender of what is being referred to.
Die auto is de jouwe. — That car is your one. That car is yours.
Dat huis is het jouwe. — That house is your one. That house is yours.
Dat is de/het jouwe. — That is your one. That is yours.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Lelystad, Flevoland, Netherlands

jol.e: Possibly from Low German and Middle Low German jolle (“dinghy”), possibly ultimately from a Proto-Germanic derivative of Proto-Indo-European *h₂ewlos (“tube”), see also Lithuanian aulas, Norwegian aul, Hittite [script needed] (auli-, “tube-shaped organ in the neck”), Albanian hollë, Latin alvus.

joviaal.e: Borrowed from French jovial, from Italian gioviale, from Latin ioviālis.

s.def: Jonah (fictional prophet who preached to the people of Nineveh, Biblical character)

jona.e: From Middle Dutch jona, from Latin Iōnās, from Koine Greek Ἰωνᾶς (Iōnâs), from Biblical Hebrew יוֹנָה (“Jonah; pigeon”).

examples: Mijn pen is leeg, kan ik de joune lenen?

joune.e: From jouwe, the standard form. The -n- was interfixed in the hiatus on the model of the pronouns mijne (“mine”) and zijn(e) (“his”), where the -n- is etymological.

examples: Hier op deze plek is ooit mijn fiets gejat.

Zoveel te doen, ik heb nog zoveel te doen: Ik moet nog eens wat jatten van een Italiaan.
 Zoveel te doen, ik heb nog zoveel te doen: Ik moet nog zwemmen in de Stille Oceaan.

s.def: plural of jat: hands, paws (the singular form is rare)
examples: Blijf met je jatten van mijn eten af!

johannesbrood.e: From Johannes + brood = St. John's bread

jordi.e: Borrowed from Catalan Jordi at the end of the 20th century.

examples:
1763, pseudonym ("A. B. C. D."), De bankbreeker door list, publ. pseudonymously ("by Fop Fopper"), page 10.
Laat Joosje naa Jooſt loopen, wat heb ik tegenwoordig met die gekheid van doen?

1784, Betje Wolff & Aagje Deken, Historie van den heer Willem Leevend, vol. 3, publ. by Isaac van Cleef, page 247.
Het is goed, dat Mama's slaapkamer verre van de zaal is; de goede Vrouw had anders ons, om ons lachen, wel naar Joost gewenscht.

joost.e: From earlier Joos, from Medieval Latin Iodocus, Iudocus, from Old Breton Iodoc. Cognate with English Joyce.

je bent wat je eet.e: A loose translation of German Der Mensch ist, was er ißt.

jeugdig.e: From Middle Dutch jodedich. Equivalent to jeugd + -ig.

s.def: iambic (consisting of iambs or characterized by their predominance)
justice (the branch of government that holds the power of jurisdiction)

justitie.e: From Middle Dutch justicie, from Latin iūstitia (possibly via Old French justise).

s.def: a TV or radio news program
a hamlet in Noord-Beveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

jacoba.e: Named for the settlement's location within the Jacobapolder and close proximity to the Jacobadijk; both locations are named after the daughter of the chief investor in the land reclamation projects which created them, Johan Pieter van den Brande.

s.def: a diminutive of the female given names Johanna or Janna
a diminutive of the female given names Josepha or Josephine
a diminutive of the female given names Josepha or Josephine

jozua.e: From older Josue, from Middle Dutch josue, from Latin Iosue, from Ancient Greek Ιωσούας (Iōsoúas), from Biblical Hebrew יְהוֹשֻׁעַ. The form with a appears in the sixteenth century, probably influenced by the Greek and Hebrew forms.

examples: Ze hebben een jarenlange vriendschap.
Na jarenlange inspanning heeft hij eindelijk zijn doel bereikt.
Hij heeft jarenlang in het buitenland gewoond.
De traditie bestaat jarenlang al in deze gemeenschap.

inflection of jonger, the comparative degree of jong:
inflection of jonger, the comparative degree of jong:

inflection of jongst, the superlative degree of jong:
inflection of jongst, the superlative degree of jong:
Javan, in, from or relating to Java, the most populous island of Indonesia
Javan, in, from or relating to the Javanese people

examples: Het Javaans, de moedertaal van de meeste Indonesiërs, is nauw verwant met het Maleis.
Wollah, pass mij die jonko.

jonko.e: Borrowed from Sranan Tongo dyonko, possibly from Twi Akan tɔ nko (“to fall asleep, to doze”).

s.def: John Q. Public, Joe Average, a hypothetical individual personification of the average (generally working-class) consumer, with an average income

jan modaal.e: Jan ('John', extremely common first name) + modaal ('average', from Latin modalis).

s.def: Jerusalem (the claimed (and de-facto) capital city of Israel; the claimed capital city of Palestine)
a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

jeruzalem.e: The neighbourhood is named after the city. The name was given in reference to the neighbourhood's white houses, which are vaguely reminiscent of houses in Jerusalem.

s.def: a sheet-shaped, primitive type of pastry, made from a baked mass of dough balls
used collectively for the sailing/nautical community

janmaat.e: Jan 'John' (extremely common Christian name) + maat '(ship) mate, sailor'

s.def: a given name to a male person or boy
a diminutive of the male given names Jan or Johannes
a diminutive of the female given name Johanna

jantje.e: From the given name Jan (Dutch equivalent of John), via its derivate jan or directly from Jan's diminutive Jantje.

s.def: a female given name, equivalent to English Josephine
a female given name, equivalent to English Josephine

examples: De hond jankte zachtjes door de pijn.
Zit hij daar te janken gelijk een klein kind!

janken.e: From Middle Dutch janken, further etymology unknown.

s.def: a male given name, equivalent to English George
alternative spelling of je van het

juweel.e: From Middle Dutch juweel, jouweel, from Anglo-Norman juel, from Old French jouel, joel, joiel, of uncertain ultimate origin. Perhaps based ultimately on Latin gaudium (“joy”), or on Latin iocus (“joke; jest”). Compare Medieval Latin jocale and English jewel.

examples: Hij is vandaag jarig.
Ben je jarig? Gefeliciteerd!

jarige job.e: From jarig + Job (a male given name).

jarige jet.e: From jarig + Jet (a female given name).

jaloers.e: From Middle Dutch jalours, jaloers, also jalous, jaloes, from Old French jalous, from Late Latin zelosus. The -r- is not etymological and does not appear in any other language; even the related Dutch term jaloezie lacks it. Its origin is unknown, but may have arisen by analogy if the endings -s a...

examples: Hij jachtte door het station om zijn trein te halen.
Ze jachtte naar de winkel voordat die sloot.

jubelen.e: From earlier jubileren, from Latin iūbilō.

jasmijn.e: From Middle Dutch jhesminne, from Middle French jasmin, from Arabic يَاسَمِين (yāsamīn), from Classical Persian یَاسَمِین (yāsamīn).

jakken.e: From Middle Dutch jakken, from Old Dutch *jakkon, from Proto-Germanic *jakkōną.

examples: Hij wil strijden tegen de hoge prijzen en voor ‘jajempies’ (borrels) van vijf cent.

jajem.e: Borrowed from Yiddish יין (yayin, “wine”), from Hebrew יַיִן (yáyin, “wine”).

s.def: second-person (gij) singular past indicative of afjagen
second-person (gij) singular past indicative of jagen
second-person (gij) singular past indicative of najagen

jammeren.e: From Middle Dutch *jammeren, iammeren, from Old Dutch *jāmaron, from Proto-West Germanic *jāmarōn, from Proto-Germanic *jēmarōną, equivalent to jammer + -en. Cognate with English yammer, German jammern.

s.def: to address someone casually by using jij, jou, je, as opposed to formal u
examples: Nu krijg ik al zakelijke brieven waarin tegen mij gejijd en gejoud wordt!

s.def: a long gun used for hunting; a hunting rifle or shotgun
to shout, to cry out, to bawl

joelen.e: A variant of jolen, from Middle Dutch jolen (“to sing loudly, cheer, rejoice”). Cognate with Middle Low German jolen (“to hoot, holler, rejoice”) (whence German Low German jöhlen (“to hoot, holler”)), German johlen (“to hoot, howl, jeer”), West Frisian joelje (“to shout”), Saterland Frisian jöälj...

s.def: John Q. Public, Joe Average, a hypothetical individual personification of the common man (or person, by extension)
examples: Gebruiksaanwijzingen moeten zo geformuleerd zijn dat Jan met de pet ze snapt.
De tweet staat vol met zulke joekels van fouten dat het niet te geloven is.

joekel.e: Borrowed from Romani ʒukel (“dog”), from Sanskrit जकुट (jakuṭa, “dog”). Attested in the sense “(large) dog” since 1873, in the broader contemporary sense since 1965.

s.def: partitive of jeugdiger, the comparative degree of jeugdig
inflection of jeugdiger, the comparative degree of jeugdig:
inflection of jeugdiger, the comparative degree of jeugdig:
inflection of jeugdiger, the comparative degree of jeugdig:
inflection of jeugdigst, the superlative degree of jeugdig:
inflection of jeugdigst, the superlative degree of jeugdig:
inflection of jeugdigst, the superlative degree of jeugdig:

jegens.e:
From Middle Dutch jegen, jegens, with unetymological -s from Old Dutch gegin, from Proto-West Germanic *gagin, from Proto-Germanic *gagin. See also tegen.
Cognate with Middle Low German jegen.

s.def: a celebrant of one's jubilee or anniversary

examples: geven (het) jawoord

s.def: alternative form of Jansen, a common surname originating as a patronymic
to try one's very best, to strive to achieve
Yalta, a city and raion in Ukraine.

examples: Coordinate term: jonkvrouw

jonkheer.e: From Middle Dutch jonchere. By surface analysis, jong (“young”) + heer (“lord”).

s.def: jugendstil, art nouveau; in particular the German, Austrian and Baltic varieties of the style

examples: Jullie hebben genoeg geld voor julliezelf

s.def: female teacher, school mistress (female teacher at a primary school, nursery school or kindergarten)

juf.e: Clipping of juffrouw. First attested in the 19th century.

s.def: God knows; I have no idea; I don't know
examples:
1951, A. Vreugdenhil, Koningen, scheepsbouwers en zeevaarders, H.J. Paris (publ.), page 151.
Waar het verschil in zit en wat de oorzaak precies is, Joost mag het weten.
Wat Hawking met dit verhaal wil bereiken, Joost mag het weten.

s.def: a Japanese-run concentration camp during World War II
one who writes books for youngsters
a female writer of books for youngsters

jeugdboekenschrijfster.e: From jeugdboeken (“books for youngsters”) + schrijfster (“female writer”).

s.def: words that are easy to understand, layman's terms
examples: En ook onze studenten boffen maar met zo'n gedreven statistiekdocent, die geen behoefte heeft om de dingen ingewikkelder te maken dan ze zijn. Jij kunt het allemaal in jip-en-janneketaal uitleggen.

Soms [laten bedrijven] een professionele tekstschrijver de code herschrijven in Jip-en-Janneketaal. Op zich is hier niets op tegen, maar het gevaar is dat daardoor de betekenis van zorgvuldig geformuleerde zinnen verandert.

jip-en-janneketaal.e: The word "jip-en-janneketaal" refers to the language (Dutch: "taal") used in Jip en Janneke, a popular series of children's books written by the Dutch author Annie M.G. Schmidt. Jip and Janneke are the two protagonists in this series. The term was coined in March 2002 by Bas Eenhoorn, chairman of...

s.def: a gown, a robe, a dress
examples: Haar japon van lichte mousseline, ietwat doorzichtig (dat was toen mode) liet de mannen niet onverschillig.
De makers hebben in totaal 248 handgemaakte strikken op de japon aangebracht.

japon.e: Clipping of japonse rok, in reference to Japan. The word was subsequently influenced by French jupon, which has influenced the Belgian pronunciation.

s.def: suffering from dementia at an unusually young age
a juniper tree; tree of the genus Juniperus
the berry of this plant; a juniper berry.

examples: In het jaarboek van de school staan foto's en herinneringen van het afgelopen schooljaar.
Het jaarboekje van de vereniging bevat een overzicht van alle activiteiten van het afgelopen jaar.
De geschiedenis van de stad wordt vastgelegd in de jaarboeken van de lokale archieven.
De jaarboeken van de vereniging geven een gedetailleerd overzicht van de gebeurtenissen en prestaties van elk jaar.

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

jet.e: A shortened form of either Henriëtte or Mariëtte.

s.def: a common surname originating as a patronymic

jansen.e: Reduced form of Janszoon, corresponding to Jan + -sen; equivalent to English Johnson.

examples: Tot in het kleinste detail speelt de band drukvol door een jaartiental van baanbrekende rockmuziek.

jaartiental.e: Equivalent to jaar (“year”) + tiental (“set of ten”). Cf. German Jahrzehnt (“decade”).

jochie.e: Dialectal diminutive of jong, equivalent to jong + -ie (diminutive suffix). Now interpreted as the diminutive of joch, which is in fact a back-formation of this word.

jurisprudentie.e: Learned borrowing from Late Latin iūrisprūdentia.

s.def: a short men’s jacket, especially one associated with sailors or workwear

jekker.e: Considered to be a derivative of jak (“upper body garment”); it could instead be a borrowing from English jacket, which would explain the initial -e-. Both are ultimately from Old French jaquet, diminutive of jaque (“tight-fitting garment”).

s.def: emphatic form of ja (“yes”), usually indicating annoyance or exasperation; yeah, yeah
examples: "Kom je nog naar beneden?" "Jaha, ik kom al."

s.def: the feeling or sentiment of youth; a youthful feeling
examples: 1983, Elseviers magazine

s.def: female teacher at a primary school or a nursing school

juffrouw.e: From Middle Dutch juffrouwe, an alternative form of joncfrouwe (from where jonkvrouw), corresponding to modern jong (“young”) + vrouw (“woman”), compare jonkheer (“esquire”).

s.def: yes man, one who habitually and uncritically agrees with anybody
Joseph, father of Jesus of Nazareth (Biblical character)
a male given name, equivalent to English Joseph

jozef.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch joseph, from Latin Iōsēphus, from Ancient Greek Ἰωσήφ (Iōsḗph), from Hebrew יוֹסֵף.

examples: De jegenode heette aanvankelijk Zoutschore (...). Rond 1100 wordt de jegenode, samen met de omgeving, ingedijkt door de Evendijk. http://www.zwinstreek.eu/202-geschiedenis/heemkundige-kringen/publicaties-heemkunde/502-het-land-achter-de-evendijk-deel-2-1963-04
The region was initially called Zoutschore (...). Around 1100 the region and its surroundings were protected by the dyke Evendijk.

jegenode.e:
From Middle Dutch jegenode, from jegen (“against”) + -ode, literally “the land which lies opposed to the observer”. Originally a calque of Old French contrée, from Vulgar Latin (terra) contrāta (“opposite land”).
Cognate to German Gegend.

examples: Robert Giebels, "De nieuwe mag het oplossen", Volkskrant, January 12, 2016.

s.def: to fib, to tell an inconsequential lie

jokken.e: From Middle Dutch jocken, from Latin iocārī (“to joke, to jest”).

jeremiëren.e: Borrowed from French jérémier. Equivalent to Jeremia + -eren.

s.def: genitive feminine/plural of jouw; of your
examples: jouwer moeder huis
het huis jouwer ouders

s.def: dative feminine of jouw; to your
examples: Ik vertelde het jouwer moeder.
Hij is jouwer niet waard.

jofel.e: Borrowed from Yiddish יפה (yofe), from Hebrew יָפֶה (yāfé).

jordy.e: Spelling variant of Jordi, of Catalan origin.

s.def: the first cash a merchant receives on a day
examples:
1903, Bernard Canter, Kalverstraat, Vennootschap ‘Letteren en Kunst’, 16.
‘Dag Dóvid. Heb je al handgift?’
‘Natuurlijk heb ik al jatmoos. In zoo'n zaak zal men geen jatmoos hebben.’

1925, Israël Querido, "Mooie Karel", in De Jordaan. Amsterdamsch epos, part 4, Scheltens & Giltay (6th ed., first ed. 1924), 278.
Soms dagen geen jatmoos, al sting je verguld achter je stal.

jatmoos.e: Borrowed from Yiddish יד מעות (yad mo('e)s). The sense “thief” is a much later development from jatmozen (“to engage in petty theft”).

s.def: honesty (any plant of the genus Lunaria)
Jesus Christ (expression of frustration, annoyance, shock or surprise)
Jewish Christianity, the religion of Christian Jews who observe Halacha (mostly used for Jewish Christian groups in antiquity)

examples:
1925, Gerard Bruning, "Bitumen (Vertelling)" (part "III. (Slot.)"), in Roeping, vol. 4, De Zuid-Nederlandse boekhandel, 292, 293.
Dien nacht, want op elke nacht-redactie ontstonden meerdere kolommen dier naamlooze en slecht gestelde journaille litteratuur, schreven de ochtendbladen zich vol aan Roger's cabaretavond.

journaille.e: Borrowed from German Journaille. Attested as early as 1925 in Dutch.

s.def: tow-line; a rope used to pull a trekschuit or other vessel

G.I. Joe, Tommy Atkins (personification of the ordinary soldier)

jengelen.e: Frequentative of janken (“to whine”), perhaps ultimately of imitative origin and related to Old French jangler (“to entertain”), as well as modern English jangle.

s.def: diminutive of jong (especially in the sense of a young animal)

examples: Geld veur te nikse en alles tax-vrij, ja, dag Jan—en maakt me nu maar iet anders wijs!

examples: (scholier): Jawadde, wee nul op tien, pff ...

jawadde.e: Equivalent to ja (“yes, yeah”) + wat (“what”). Possibly from West-Flemish Dutch.

s.def: hunting still-life, gamepiece (painting or other picture of an arrangement with dead animals)
Jagdstück (cuckoo clock with motifs related to hunting)
examples: In dat latoenkoper is het volgende tafereel te zien: bovenin overziet een engel, met waarschijnlijk een druiventros in de hand, wat er onder de wijzerplaat gebeurt. Een jachthond aan de rechterzijde, een haas aan de linker. Een van de laatste jachtklokken.

s.def: alternative form of Jansen, a common surname originating as a patronymic
a town that is an important producer of gin, especially of jenever

jeneverstad.e: 19th century. Compound of jenever + stad. After Schiedam's high production of jenever since the mid-eighteenth century.

jammerpoel.e: Compound of jammer (“suffering”) + poel (“puddle, abyss, hell”).

joppe.e: First attested as Jobstede in 1608. Compound of Job (a personal name) and Middle Dutch stede (“place, city”).

jammervol.e: Compound of jammeren or jammer + vol.

A familiar form for Joop, Jakob, Johannes, etc.

jolen.e: From Middle Dutch jolen (“to sing loudly, rejoice, exult”), of uncertain origin and affiliation, but apparently related to Middle Low German jolen, jȫlen (“to cheer, exult, jubilate”), Middle High German jōlen ("to sing loudly"; > German johlen). Perhaps related to Dutch joelen (“to cry aloud, ba...

s.def: Jewish Christian (typically used for early Christian groups like the Ebionites)

jodenchristen.e: Compound of jood (“Jew”) + -en- + christen (“Christian”), possibly a calque of German Judenchrist. First attested around 1850.

je-weet-welkater.e: From "je weet wel": 'you know?' and kater: 'tomcat': term originally from the Dutch comic strip "Jan, Jans en de kinderen", "Jack, Jacky and the Juniors" in English.

s.def: a young male noble, often lacking a knighthood or a noble title of his own
Title of address for midshipmen and cadets.

jonker.e: From Middle Dutch joncker. Equivalent to a reduction of jonkheer.

s.def: Expression of disgust or revulsion: yuck, ew, bah
a male given name from Dutch, corresponding to Jakob and Johannes, also to Jozef in the South
Personification of a slapdash, unprofessional person.

jan boerenfluitjes.e: First attested in the early 20th century. The phrase op zijn boerenfluitjes may predate the personification Jan Boerenfluitjes.

s.def: marina (recreational harbour for yachts and small boats)
examples: De jachthaven was vol met luxe boten.
Ze legden aan in een kleine jachthaven aan de kust.

s.def: A mild exclamation of (perhaps unpleasant) surprise, wonder or amazement.

jeempie.e: Alteration of jemig, itself a euphemistic alteration of Jezus.

s.def: euphemistic form of Jezus, indicating (mostly unpleasant) surprise, wonder or amazement
Judas kiss (treacherous or ingenuine act done under the pretense of kindness)
examples:
2007, Maite Carranza, De ijswoestijn, tr. by E. Veenings (Spanish title El desierto de hielo), The War of the Witches series vol. 2, Signature (publ.).
Ze liep de bioscoop in zonder Selene een kus te geven, want dat zou een judaskus zijn geweest.

2008, Val McDermid, Het profiel, tr. by Sophie Brinkman (English title The Wire In The Blood), Tony Hill series vol. 2, Sijthoff (publ.).
Alle vooruitgang die hij in het afgelopen jaar had gemaakt werd plotseling ongedaan gemaakt, en weer rook hij de geur van zweet en bloed, voelde zijn glibberige handen, hoorde de schreeuwen die uit zijn eigen keel werden losgescheurd en proefde de judaskus.

judaskus.e: Compound of Judas + kus. After Judas's kiss in Mark 14,45 and parallels. The same metaphor is used in several other European languages.

examples: voor jou en de jouwen

jachtvliegtuig.e: Compound of jacht + vliegtuig. Compare German Jagdflugzeug.

s.def: a tow line, a rope used to tug a boat (by someone or something on land)
to grab (someone) by the hands and feet and swing or throw (him or her) upward repeatedly, done as a game or as hazing
genitive masculine/neuter of jouw; of your
examples: jouws vaders huis
het huis jouws vaders
Hij is jouws niet waard.

s.def: Negative personification of the supposedly unenterprising, unpatriotic (insufficiently chauvinist and colonialist), conservative 19th century Dutch mentality.

jan salie.e: Coined by Everhardus Johannes Potgieter. From salie.

s.def: Expression of disgust or revulsion: yuck, ew, bah

jakkie.e: From jakkes, from jasses, from Jezus.

s.def: justiciable, subject or accountable to (a particular type or domain of) law

Javan, a person or native from Java
a female celebrant of her jubilee or anniversary
volume (all issues of a periodical published in one year)
examples: Ik heb de volledige jaargang 2020 van dat tijdschrift.
Deze bibliotheek heeft jaargangen van kranten die teruggaan tot de 18e eeuw.

jopper.e: Borrowed from German Joppe, from Middle Low German joppe, from Old Italian giuppa, from Old Spanish aljuba, from Andalusian Arabic جبة (gubbah), from Arabic جبة (“cloak, coat”).

a neighbourhood of Zwijndrecht, South Holland, Netherlands

julianadorp.e:
* (Noord-Holland) First attested as School in 1867. Compound of Juliana + dorp. The village is named after Queen Juliana of the Netherlands. The village received this name in 1909 at the request of Pieter Loopuyt, a descendant of the settlement's founder.
* (Curaçao) Compound of Juliana (a person...

s.def: a town and former municipality of De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

joure.e: First attested as jeure in the 13th century. Etymology unknown. Potentially derived from the ancient hydronym *Gebharô or (less probably) from West Frisian hjouwer (“oat (Avena sativa)”). Compare Hiaure, De Joere and De Jouwer. See also West Frisian De Jouwer.

jagerslatijn.e: From jager + -s- + Latijn, calque of German Jägerlatein.

s.def: a hamlet in Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

jonen.e: First attested as JONEN, Joene, 't Joone in 1844. Etymology unknown. Not related to joon (“beacon buoy”).

s.def: alternative form of Jansen, a common surname originating as a patronymic
a nasty and furtive deed, a vile trick

jezuïetenstreek.e: Compound of jezuïet + -en- + streek. Inspired by Protestant and anticlerical stereotypes about Jesuits, who were considered the archetypical Roman Catholic missionaries.

s.def: a person with Down's syndrome, a person with mental retardation

josti.e: Clipping of Jostiband Orkest (“Jostiband Orchestra”), wherein Josti is a blend of Johannes + Stichting (“Foundation”).

jazzvogel.e: Compound of jazz (“jazz”) + vogel (“dude, cat; bird”).

s.def: a yearling, notably an equine or bovine animal aged between one and two
examples: De boer heeft een kudde jaarlingen die volgend jaar volwassen zullen zijn.
De fokker heeft een paar veelbelovende jaarlingen die in de toekomst hopelijk goede renpaarden zullen worden.

s.def: An account covering a calendar year
the annual accounts of a firm or other public or private, accountable organisation; often part of an annual report
examples: De jaarrekening van het bedrijf toont een sterke groei.
De accountant is verantwoordelijk voor het opstellen van de jaarrekening.
Je kunt de leeftijd van een boom tellen aan de hand van de jaarringen.
Elke jaarring vertegenwoordigt een jaar in het leven van de boom.

s.def: analogous layer in continuously growing animal organs, as on scales or horns
an annual yield, either in a given year or per annum
an economic entity's annual turnover, either in a given year or per annum
examples: De jaaromzet van het bedrijf steeg met 10% ten opzichte van vorig jaar.

s.def: an annual production, output either in a given year or per annum
examples: De jaarproductie van deze fabriek bedraagt 100.000 auto's per jaar.

jonggezel.e:
From jong + gezel.
Cognate with Luxembourgish Jonggesell.

s.def: transition from one year to the next
change from one year to the next

jodin.e: From Middle Dutch jodinne. Equivalent to jood + -in.

s.def: a yacht (light, fast vessel), in particular a sailing yacht

jachtschip.e: Compound of jacht (“haste”) or jachten (“to hurry”) + schip (“ship”). Perhaps a modification of Middle Dutch jageschip. Although jachtschip is attested later than jacht (“yacht”), it is often supposed to be the older form; for one, it explains the neuter gender of jacht.

s.def: a large, flat, round, sweet biscuit similar to shortbread

jodenkoek.e: Compound of jood + -en- + koek. Some sources state that early types of pastry with the name, some with considerably different recipes, were originally produced by Jewish bakers.

s.def: Jacob's ladder, Greek valerian, Polemonium caeruleum

jucht.e: Borrowed from Middle Low German juften, from Russian юфть (juftʹ).

juveniel.e: Borrowed from French juvénile, from Latin iuvenīlis.

examples: 'Meneer Duyn, u bent lid van de Tweede Kamer?' 'Jazeker.'
'Ga nu weg.' 'Jazeker, meneer G.'

s.def: a crybaby, one who weeps or complains
persecution of Jews (prescriptively, particularly as an ethnic or cultural group)
persecution of Jews (prescriptively, particularly as a religious group)
Judaean (from, of or pertaining to Judaea)

judees.e: From Middle Dutch judeesch. Equivalent to Judea + -ees.

s.def: a castle or other grand (secondary) residence in the country, fit for hunting
a yes man, a male sycophant or weathervane
sin of one's youth, sin of youth

jeugdvriendin.e: Compound of jeugd (“youth, childhood”) + vriendin (“female friend”).

examples: We moeten vanavond naar buiten. Is dat oké met jou? — Jahoor, dat is oké!
Jahoor, ik ben nog goed in vorm.

jaarhonderd.e: From jaar + honderd, calque of German Jahrhundert.

s.def: a youth prison, special penitentiary for juvenile delinquents, typically with a mild regime focussed on (re)education
a number representing a year (according to an era), notably as (part of) a date
examples: Het jaartal 1776 is belangrijk in de Amerikaanse geschiedenis.
Controleer het jaartal op de fles wijn.

s.def: a diminutive of the female given name Jeanne
a neighbourhood of Venlo, Limburg, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Beekdaelen, Limburg, Netherlands

jabeek.e:
First attested as apud Iabeche in 1144. Compound of an unclear first element and beek (“brook, stream”). The first element has been interpreted as gat (“hole, passageway”) and as a prehistoric hydronym *Jatia. Compare Jabbeke.
See also Limburgish Jaobik.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Maastricht, Limburg, Netherlands

jekerkwartier.e:
Compound of the hydronym Jeker and kwartier (“quarter”).
See also Limburgish Jekerkerteer.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Maastricht, Limburg, Netherlands

jekerdal.e: Compound of the hydronym Jeker and dal (“dale, valley”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Meierijstad, North Brabant, Netherlands

jekschot.e: First attested as yecschot and yecscot in 1311. Etymology unknown. Compound of an unknown first element and schoot (“piece of land that juts upward”) or schot (“enclosed area, fenced-in land”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Lelystad, Flevoland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Ommen, Overijssel, Netherlands

junne.e: First attested as junne in 1381-1383. Etymology unknown.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Dinkelland, Overijssel, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Bergen op Zoom, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Midden-Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

jagerswijk.e: First attested as Iaauwers wijck in 1680. Originally a compound of the toponym Joure and wijk (“secondary canal in a turf extraction area”). The first element was gradually reinterpreted to jager (“hunter”).

s.def: a village in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

jipsingboertange.e: First attested as Jispen Boeren Tange Weg in 1851-1855. Compound of the toponym Jipsinghuizen, boer (“inhabitant”) and dialectal tange (“sandy ridge in a raised bog”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

jipsingboermussel.e: First attested as in de Jipseboure-Mussel around 1750. Compound of the toponym Jipsinghuizen, boer (“inhabitant”) and mussel (“swampland”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

jipsinghuizen.e: First attested as Iipsinghuijsen in 1634. Compound of Jipsing (a surname) and the plural form of huis (“house”). Compare Ellersinghuizen and Wollinghuizen.

s.def: a village in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

jukwerd.e: First attested as iucawerth in the 13th century. Compound of Old Frisian Jukke (a personal name) and wart (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a village in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

jonkersvaart.e: First attested as Jonkersgruppel in 1765. Compound of jonker (“squire”) and vaart (“canal”). Exactly which squire ordered the construction of the canal is uncertain.

s.def: a hamlet in Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

jachtveld.e: Calque of West Frisian It Jachtfjild, derived in turn from jachtfjild (“hunting field”).

s.def: a village in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

janum.e: First attested as Jawnum in 1506. Borrowed from West Frisian Jannum, which may derive from a compound of Old Frisian Iwe (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”). The first element is unlikely to be Jan, as Christian names were introduced to Frisian in a time when the hēm element was no lon...

s.def: a village in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

jislum.e: First attested as gisleheim in 944. Borrowed from West Frisian Jislum, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Gisilo (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a village in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

jouswier.e: First attested as juusweer in 1495. Borrowed from West Frisian Jouswier, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Jou (a personal name) or Jouwe and wier (“artificial mound used for habitation”). Compare Jousterp and Jouswerd.

s.def: a village in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

jellum.e: First attested as helmum in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Jellum, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Helm (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

jorwerd.e: First attested as everwerth in 1221. Borrowed from West Frisian Jorwert, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Ever (a personal name) and werth (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

jelsum.e: First attested as heilsum around 1270. Borrowed from West Frisian Jelsum, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Helle (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a village in Opsterland, Friesland, Netherlands

jonkersland.e: First attested as Jonkersland ('t) in 1899. Calque of West Frisian Jonkerslân, a compound of jonker (“squire”) and lân (“land”). Probably named after Juw Dekama.

s.def: a village in Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

jubbega.e: First attested as jobbegae in 1408. Derived from West Frisian Jobbegea, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Jobbe (a personal name) and gâ (“village, village area”).

s.def: a hamlet in Ooststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

janssenstichting.e: Attested as P.W. Janssenstichting in 1958. Compound of Janssen (a surname) and stichting (“foundation”). Named after Peter Wilhelm Janssen, an entrepreneur and philanthropist who founded several social organisations and developed land in the Ooststellingwerf area.

s.def: a hamlet in Ooststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

jardinga.e: First attested as Jardingermark in 1543. Derived from West Frisian Jardinga (a surname).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

jabikswoude.e: Compound of the Frisian form of the name of Saint Jacob and woud (“wood”). The neighbourhood is located close to the Frisian stretch of the Camino de Santiago.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

jan van scorelbuurt.e: Compound of the name of Jan van Scorel and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after famous Renaissance artists.

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

jutrijp.e: First attested as ryp in 1482. Borrowed from West Frisian Jutryp, derived in turn from a compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian ripe (“edge, bank”). The first element may be Jort (a personal name) or iet (“hole”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

jonkershuizen.e: First attested as buwanda in 1478. Calque of West Frisian Jonkershuzen, a compound of jonker (“squire”) and the plural form of hûs (“house”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

jousterp.e: First attested as Yoersterp in 1543. Borrowed from West Frisian Jousterp, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Jorre (a personal name), Jou or Jouwe and terp (“artificial mound used for habitation”). Compare Jouswerd and Jouswier.

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

jouswerd.e: First attested as Jowswerd and Jouswird in 1511. Borrowed from West Frisian Jouswert, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Jou (a personal name) or Jouwe and werd (“artificial mound used for habitation”). Compare Jousterp and Jouswier.

s.def: a former village and former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

jacobswoude.e: The municipality was named after the former village.

jan sofat.e: Borrowed from Papiamentu Jan Sofat, derived from Jan (a personal name) and zoutvat (“saltshaker, salt barrel”). Originally the name of a plantation.

jan thiel.e: Allegedly derived from the name of the owner of the plantation which the neighbourhood developed around.

jan donker.e: Originally the name of a plantation named after its first owner.

jan kok.e: Derived from the name of the first owner of the plantation which the village developed around.

jongbloed.e: Originally the name of a plantation.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Sint Eustatius, Netherlands
Jordan (a river in West Asia in the Middle East, that empties into the Dead Sea, flowing through Israel, the Golan Heights, the West Bank and Jordan)
a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

jordaan.e: Attested as Jordane in 1691. The use of this name for the neighborhood dates to the 17th century, when the Prinsengracht canal was first constructed, which was at that time apparently jokingly referred to by the name of the Jordan river. The neighborhood that was built beyond the canal around tha...

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

java-eiland.e: Compound of the toponym Java and eiland (“island”). Named after the island in Indonesia.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

jan maijenbuurt.e: Compound of the name of captain Jan Jacobszoon May van Schellinkhout and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Wormerland, North Holland, Netherlands

jisp.e: First attested as gispe in 1328. Derived from a hydronym, derived in turn from Proto-Germanic *jesan (“ferment”) and Old Dutch *apa (“river, watercourse”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zaanstad, North Holland, Netherlands

j. j. allanstraat.e: First attested as crabbelbuyert in 1564. Compound of the name of Johannes Jacobus Allan (1862-1933) and straat (“street”). The hamlet is named after the first bus driver of the Zaan region.

s.def: a hamlet in Leusden, Utrecht, Netherlands

jannendorp.e: Attested as Jannendorp in 1936. Compound of an unclear first element and dorp (“village, settlement”). The first element may be dialectal jannen (“chestnut, Castanea sativa”) used in a perjorative sense to refer to the perceived poverty of the settlement's inhabitants. Compare Musschendorp.

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Nieuwegein, Utrecht, Netherlands

jutphaas.e:
First attested as iudifax in 1165. Etymology unknown.
Possibly a compound of iūdicō (“judicial”) + fax (“hair, head of hair”) or Latinized -fahs, as a prefix or suffix found in toponyms meaning an area or plot of short, rough grass. Compare Halifax.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Lopik, Utrecht, Netherlands

jaarsveld.e: First attested as jaarsfelt in 1331. Compound of Middle Dutch Jaar (a personal name) and velt (“field”).

s.def: Interjection affirming something someone else said.
examples: A: Dat feest was echt nice. B: Ja toch, was echt dik.

jan-van-gent.e: Derived from gent (“male goose”), but the origin of the initial two syllables remains unexplained. Superficially, the name means "John of Ghent", but gent here is unrelated to the name of the Belgian city.

s.def: age of reason (age at which a person is able to distinguish right from wrong and make their own decisions)

examples: Het uur, de jadders of elders, de melkborsten en prammen der Koeijen vereiſchen hier een nadere beſchrijving.

jadder.e:
Borrowed from Dutch Low Saxon [Term?]. Doublet of uier.
In the past sometimes used in standard Dutch writing, particularly following its use by the seventeenth-century playwright Joost van den Vondel.

s.def: synonym of rozenkever (“garden chafer, June bug, Phyllopertha horticola”)
as the twig is bent, so is the tree inclined

jong geleerd is oud gedaan.e: Literally, “learned when young means done when old”.

s.def: young adult (having an age approximately between 18-30)
gravy spoon, small ladle for serving gravy or sauces

jongstleden.e: From jongst + leden, where jongst is the superlative degree of jong (“young”), and leden is an obsolete past participle of lijden, with the obsolete sense of "to pass (of time)".

s.def: a highly unfavorable transaction, daylight robbery
examples: Witheet is Mieke Krottje, nadat ze in Deventer via een parkeerapp ten onrechte 50 euro betaalde voor nauwelijks drie minuten parkeren. Ze verwacht dat veel meer mensen de klos waren. "Dit is je reinste diefstal."

s.def: a card game similar to Rummikub
to play the card game of jokeren

jeetjemineetje.e: Rhyming variant of jeetjemina, from jee, a minced oath of Jezus.

examples: Jeugdlonen zijn in veel landen achtergebleven.

s.def: a minimum wage paid to people below 21 years of age, which is significantly lower than the minimum wage paid to adults at age 21 or older
examples: De vakbond ageert tegen het discriminatoire jeugdloon.

jakobskruiskruid.e: From Jakob (“Jacob”) + -s- + kruiskruid (“plant currently or formerly of genus Senecio”).

jutten.e: Likely a back-formation from strandjutten, from the noun strandjut, itself probably a derogatory term derived from Jut (“Jute, inhabitant of Jutland”). Alternatively, it has been viewed as a by-form of jatten (“to steal”), which is of Yiddish origin.

jodenrein.e: Compound of Jood + -en- + rein, a calque of German judenrein.

s.def: An eager and energetic young newcomer.
examples: De tegendraadse jonge hond veroorzaakte furore in de partij.

s.def: Used other than figuratively or idiomatically: see jong, hond.

jonge hond.e: Literally, “young dog”. Compare Polish młody wilk.

examples: Op de Jaardag van het Korps Rijdende Artillerie (elk jaar rond de 21e februari) vieren de officieren en onderofficieren van het KRA de verjaardag van het KRA (21 februari 1793).

jaardag.e: From Middle Dutch jaerdach, from Old Dutch *jārdag, from Proto-West Germanic *jāradag, from Proto-Germanic *jēradagaz, equivalent to jaar + dag.

s.def: special channel on social media, often on Instagram or YouTube, on which gossip is shared

juicekanaal.e: From juice + kanaal, calque of English juice channel.

jouwent.e: From jouwen (dative masculine/neuter of jouw), with a paragogic t.

jouwerzijds.e: Calque of German deinerseits. By surface analysis, jouwer + zijde + -s.

s.def: hallmark indicating the year of assaying by means of a letter
examples: Een paar zeer zeldzame Arnhemse sauskommen door Daniël Courou, jaarletter X, circa 1750.
"Er zitten drie merken in: ons meesterteken 'VKB', het jaarmerk - een letter - en het waarborgstempel dat garant staat voor het zilvergehalte. Iedereen ziet zo dat het uit onze fabriek komt."
Naast gehaltemerken, slaat men jaartekens, kantoormerken van het waarborgkantoor dat de keuring uitvoert, en in sommige gevallen dat van de maker, het zogenaamde meesterteken.

s.def: a juniper tree; tree of the genus Juniperus
the berry of this plant; a juniper berry.
the berry of Ribes nigrum, a black currant
